name: C(12,6,4) checkpoint lifecycle canary
hypothesis: Two bounded exact 41-cover checks survive a durable review boundary and produce a final completion event.
expected_signal: Segment 1 records 1/2 correct with checkpoint; after continue, segment 2 records 2/2 complete; both bind the known witness.
decision_value: Proves queue, bounded worker, checkpoint and review continuation, durable completion event, and post-completion review on real C evidence before expensive SAT.

efficiency_design.naive_cost: Treat timer enablement as sufficient and wait for an unobserved research job to expose queue, checkpoint, completion-event, and review failures.
opportunities_considered: symmetry reduction, canonical augmentation, and orbit quotients
bitsets, batching, vectorization, and incremental evaluation
memoization, caching, reusable clauses, and reusable proof prefixes
decomposition, cube-and-conquer, meet-in-the-middle, and resumable segments
compressed artifacts and streamed or hash-verified manifests
sound cheap prefilters and independently checked coverage
efficiency_design.chosen_reductions: Use the repository's 41-block control witness and direct exact checker as a tiny deterministic payload, split into two identical bounded segments so the first checkpoint requires a continuation review and the second creates a final completion event.
efficiency_design.expected_throughput_gain: The full lifecycle is exercised in seconds with negligible artifact growth, instead of consuming a real SAT tranche merely to test orchestration.
efficiency_design.soundness_basis: Each segment independently enumerates all 495 four-subsets through checkers/verify_cover.py, requires exactly 41 distinct six-blocks, and records correctness and decision-value gates in durable JSON.
efficiency_design.remains_uncompressed: This canary verifies orchestration and the known 41-block positive control only. It does not test solver proof production, close any 40-block frontier node, or support an exact-value claim.
